title: The ACLU Is Arming Lawyers to Expose State Surveillance Secrets
excerpt: A new toolkit for attorneys in Massachusetts targets the technologies police use—and conceal—to build criminal cases, from facial recognition to AI-written police reports.
content_html:
The American Civil Liberties Union of Massachusetts says it’s releasing an online toolkit this week for criminal defense attorneys designed to uncover whether police used surveillance technologies—facial recognition, automatic license plate readers, gunshot detection systems, and more—to build the cases against their clients in secret.

The toolkit, which the group describes as the first of its kind, is built around handcrafted legal motions that, when granted by a judge, will force prosecutors to reveal whether surveillance technology was used against a defendant. It covers an array of technologies, from “stingray” phone trackers and location data sold by commercial brokers to AI-drafted police reports and forensic tools that crack phones and siphon data from car infotainment systems.

It also includes preservation motions—demands that surveillance data be saved before it is deleted automatically—aimed at government agencies and private vendors alike. The filings rest on a bedrock rule of American criminal law, established in 1963, that prosecutors are required to turn over all evidence that could help the defense. The motions will be distributed through a password-protected library, the ACLU of Massachusetts says, available only to verified defense attorneys.

The group is fighting a war against unregulated surveillance tools on two fronts, says Jennifer Herrmann, a staff attorney at the ACLU of Massachusetts and one of the toolkit’s authors. One front is pressuring cities to drop tools like Flock, the license plate reader network, and ShotSpotter, the gunshot detection system, outright. The other is arming defense lawyers to expose the surveillance in court.

“The amount of time that it can take for that initial use of technology and investigation to actually get to a court and constitutional challenge is long,” Herrmann says—so the motions give attorneys a way to “challenge what has already happened in the past,” building a record that carries over to whatever tool comes next.

The project responds to what its authors describe as a chronic lag between the moment police adopt a new surveillance tool and the moment courts finally decide whether using it is legal. Most Americans carried cell phones by the late 1990s, but the United States Supreme Court did not require police to get a warrant for cell phone location records until 2018. The lag isn’t just the result of slow-moving courts. The technologies with the worst disclosure records have faced the fewest court rulings precisely because a defendant cannot challenge a tool they were never told about.

In materials accompanying the launch, the ACLU of Massachusetts says police deploy these tools “sometimes without even disclosing their use to the prosecutors handling the case.” For years, the FBI required local police departments to sign secrecy agreements before using cell-site simulators—suitcase-sized devices, commonly called stingrays, that impersonate cell towers to secretly locate phones. In Baltimore, one such agreement instructed prosecutors to drop criminal cases entirely rather than reveal the device in court, even as the city’s police used it more than 4,300 times between 2007 and 2015. FBI records later obtained by the ACLU show the agreements persisted until at least 2020.

When Missouri investigators used Fog Reveal, a commercial tool that maps people’s movements using app-harvested location data, in the investigation of a 2017 murder, the Associated Press found no trace of it in the trial exhibits that helped convict the victim’s wife. And when Louisiana authorities obtained an arrest warrant in 2022 for Randal Quran Reid—a Georgia man misidentified by facial recognition and jailed for nearly a week—the warrant attributed his identification not to software but to “a credible source,” according to The Washington Post. The very document that authorized his arrest actively obscured how police came to suspect him.

The toolkit’s authors frame the problem slightly differently: Their central worry is not only evidence hidden from prosecutors but defendants who are never told a surveillance tool touched their case at all, and defense lawyers who don't know to ask.

Gideon Epstein, a Technology for Liberty policy counsel at the civil liberties group and the toolkit’s other author, points to Massachusetts's own leading license plate reader case, Commonwealth v. McCarthy, in which the defense sought records for only a handful of cameras rather than the statewide network. “A lot of the time they're not asking for the full scope of surveillance technologies in use,” Epstein says. The motions, he adds, are as much about prompting lawyers to demand everything as about forcing the government to cough it up.

The preservation motions target a separate and subtler problem: surveillance evidence that destroys itself. Axon's Draft One, an AI tool that writes police reports from body camera audio, discards the machine-written first draft by design. An Axon product manager told the Electronic Frontier Foundation in July 2025 that the choice avoids creating “disclosure headaches” for police customers and prosecutors. California legislators saw the matter differently and enacted a law last year that requires agencies to keep the original drafts.

Elsewhere, Flock Safety’s license plate reader network deletes its footage after 30 days by default, and Chicago’s contract for the ShotSpotter gunshot detection system declared the gunfire data the vendor's own property, not the city’s. In each case, the digital material a defendant would need to fight back against a claim can vanish—or sit beyond the government's reach—before a defense attorney is ever appointed to the case.

Flock Safety spokesperson Paris Lewbel says the company has procedures to preserve the integrity and authenticity of data stored in its system, and tools to confirm that an ALPR image or investigative report has not been altered since it was pulled from the platform.

Lewbel says Flock complies with legal demands for customer data. Requests that don't legally bind Flock it refers to the customer, whom it also notifies.

A spokesperson for SoundThinking, maker of ShotSpotter, says the company complies with subpoenas and court orders regardless of which side issues them, and cooperates with “reasonable requests” for data in criminal proceedings. When a subpoena covers material the company considers confidential, such as its own trade secrets, it asks courts to restrict that material's dissemination.

Epstein is careful to note the ACLU still generally wants this data to quickly disappear. It’s lobbying Massachusetts to cut license plate reader retention to as little as two days, from Flock's default of 30. But once someone is facing charges, he says, that same data can be the evidence that clears them, which is what the preservation motions are designed to protect.

If the approach sounds theoretical, Massachusetts's highest court has recently proven otherwise, twice. In 2024, the state’s Supreme Judicial Court permanently threw out drug and firearm charges against a Boston man—barring prosecutors from ever refiling them—after police defied a court order to turn over records of fake social media profiles that officers used to surveil him, built with predominantly Black and brown avatars. Last year, the same court ruled that defendants can demand a hearing forcing prosecutors to prove ShotSpotter actually works before its evidence reaches a jury. The rulings help explain why the toolkit is built on Massachusetts law, which the ACLU says can be adapted for other states: The state’s high court has run years ahead of the US Supreme Court on digital privacy, requiring warrants for cell phone location data back in 2014.

The ACLU’s timing is not incidental: Three weeks ago, the US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that when police use a so-called geofence warrant—an order forcing Google to identify virtually all phones near a crime scene—they are conducting a “search” under the Fourth Amendment, triggering the Constitution's privacy protections. The justices sent the case back down for lower courts to work out what a valid geofence warrant looks like. The ACLU’s toolkit will ship a geofencing motion directly into those unsettled waters.

The motions are meant to be adapted and filed in real criminal cases, and the group is asking attorneys who use them to report back. The point, Herrmann says, is to take good lawyering that already exists and put it “at the fingertips of every attorney for every defendant,” while continuing to flag the gaps where new strategies are needed.

Updated at 1:10 pm ET, July 20, 2026: Added comments from Flock Safety and SoundThinking.

blocks.html_en: The American Civil Liberties Union of Massachusetts says it’s releasing an online toolkit this week for criminal defense attorneys designed to uncover whether police used surveillance technologies—facial recognition, automatic license plate readers, gunshot detection systems, and more—to build the cases against their clients in secret.

sentences.en: The American Civil Liberties Union of Massachusetts says it’s releasing an online toolkit this week for criminal defense attorneys designed to uncover whether police used surveillance technologies—facial recognition, automatic license plate readers, gunshot detection systems, and more—to build the cases against their clients in secret.
sentences.zh: 马萨诸塞州美国公民自由联盟表示，本周将向刑事辩护律师发布一个在线工具包，旨在揭露警方是否使用监控技术（面部识别、自动车牌读取器、枪击检测系统等）来秘密针对其客户立案。

blocks.html_en: The toolkit, which the group describes as the first of its kind, is built around handcrafted legal motions that, when granted by a judge, will force prosecutors to reveal whether surveillance technology was used against a defendant. It covers an array of technologies, from “stingray” phone trackers and location data sold by commercial brokers to AI-drafted police reports and forensic tools that crack phones and siphon data from car infotainment systems.

sentences.zh: 该组织称该工具包是同类中的第一个工具包，它是围绕手工制作的法律动议构建的，当法官批准这些动议时，将迫使检察官披露是否对被告使用了监控技术。

The motions will be distributed through a password-protected library, the ACLU of Massachusetts says, available only to verified defense attorneys.

The other is arming defense lawyers to expose the surveillance in court.

sentences.zh: 保存动议针对的是一个单独且更微妙的问题：监视证据会自我毁灭。

sentences.en: Axon's Draft One, an AI tool that writes police reports from body camera audio, discards the machine-written first draft by design.
sentences.zh: Axon 的 Draft One 是一款人工智能工具，可以根据随身摄像机的音频编写警方报告，在设计上抛弃了机器编写的初稿。

sentences.en: An Axon product manager told the Electronic Frontier Foundation in July 2025 that the choice avoids creating “disclosure headaches” for police customers and prosecutors.
sentences.zh: 2025 年 7 月，Axon 的一位产品经理告诉电子前沿基金会，这一选择可以避免给警方客户和检察官带来“披露难题”。

sentences.en: California legislators saw the matter differently and enacted a law last year that requires agencies to keep the original drafts.
sentences.zh: 加州立法者对此事有不同的看法，并于去年颁布了一项法律，要求各机构保留原始草案。

blocks.html_en: Elsewhere, Flock Safety’s license plate reader network deletes its footage after 30 days by default, and Chicago’s contract for the ShotSpotter gunshot detection system declared the gunfire data the vendor's own property, not the city’s. In each case, the digital material a defendant would need to fight back against a claim can vanish—or sit beyond the government's reach—before a defense attorney is ever appointed to the case.

sentences.en: Elsewhere, Flock Safety’s license plate reader network deletes its footage after 30 days by default, and Chicago’s contract for the ShotSpotter gunshot detection system declared the gunfire data the vendor's own property, not the city’s.
sentences.zh: 在其他地方，Flock Safety 的车牌读取器网络默认会在 30 天后删除其录像，而芝加哥的 ShotSpotter 枪击检测系统合同则声明枪击数据是供应商自己的财产，而不是该市的财产。

sentences.en: In each case, the digital material a defendant would need to fight back against a claim can vanish—or sit beyond the government's reach—before a defense attorney is ever appointed to the case.
sentences.zh: 在每种情况下，在指定辩护律师处理案件之前，被告反击索赔所需的数字材料可能会消失，或者超出政府的控制范围。

blocks.html_en: Flock Safety spokesperson Paris Lewbel says the company has procedures to preserve the integrity and authenticity of data stored in its system, and tools to confirm that an ALPR image or investigative report has not been altered since it was pulled from the platform.

sentences.en: Flock Safety spokesperson Paris Lewbel says the company has procedures to preserve the integrity and authenticity of data stored in its system, and tools to confirm that an ALPR image or investigative report has not been altered since it was pulled from the platform.
sentences.zh: Flock Safety 发言人 Paris Lewbel 表示，该公司拥有保护系统中存储数据完整性和真实性的程序，以及确认 ALPR 图像或调查报告自从平台上撤下后未被更改的工具。

blocks.html_en: Lewbel says Flock complies with legal demands for customer data. Requests that don't legally bind Flock it refers to the customer, whom it also notifies.

sentences.en: Lewbel says Flock complies with legal demands for customer data.
sentences.zh: Lewbel 表示 Flock 遵守对客户数据的法律要求。

sentences.en: Requests that don't legally bind Flock it refers to the customer, whom it also notifies.
sentences.zh: 不具有法律约束力的请求 Flock 会向客户发出通知，并通知客户。

blocks.html_en: A spokesperson for SoundThinking, maker of ShotSpotter, says the company complies with subpoenas and court orders regardless of which side issues them, and cooperates with “reasonable requests” for data in criminal proceedings. When a subpoena covers material the company considers confidential, such as its own trade secrets, it asks courts to restrict that material's dissemination.

sentences.en: A spokesperson for SoundThinking, maker of ShotSpotter, says the company complies with subpoenas and court orders regardless of which side issues them, and cooperates with “reasonable requests” for data in criminal proceedings.
sentences.zh: ShotSpotter 制造商 SoundThinking 的发言人表示，无论哪一方发出传票和法院命令，该公司都会遵守传票和法院命令，并配合刑事诉讼中索取数据的“合理要求”。

sentences.en: When a subpoena covers material the company considers confidential, such as its own trade secrets, it asks courts to restrict that material's dissemination.
sentences.zh: 当传票涵盖公司认为机密的材料（例如其自己的商业秘密）时，它会要求法院限制该材料的传播。

blocks.html_en: Epstein is careful to note the ACLU still generally wants this data to quickly disappear. It’s lobbying Massachusetts to cut license plate reader retention to as little as two days, from Flock's default of 30. But once someone is facing charges, he says, that same data can be the evidence that clears them, which is what the preservation motions are designed to protect.

sentences.en: Epstein is careful to note the ACLU still generally wants this data to quickly disappear.
sentences.zh: 爱泼斯坦谨慎地指出，美国公民自由联盟仍然普遍希望这些数据迅速消失。

sentences.en: It’s lobbying Massachusetts to cut license plate reader retention to as little as two days, from Flock's default of 30.
sentences.zh: 它正在游说马萨诸塞州将车牌读取器的保留时间从 Flock 的默认保留时间 30 天缩短到两天。

sentences.en: But once someone is facing charges, he says, that same data can be the evidence that clears them, which is what the preservation motions are designed to protect.
sentences.zh: 但他表示，一旦有人面临指控，同样的数据就可以成为洗清罪名的证据，而这正是保存动议旨在保护的内容。

blocks.html_en: If the approach sounds theoretical, Massachusetts's highest court has recently proven otherwise, twice. In 2024, the state’s Supreme Judicial Court permanently threw out drug and firearm charges against a Boston man—barring prosecutors from ever refiling them—after police defied a court order to turn over records of fake social media profiles that officers used to surveil him, built with predominantly Black and brown avatars. Last year, the same court ruled that defendants can demand a hearing forcing prosecutors to prove ShotSpotter actually works before its evidence reaches a jury. The rulings help explain why the toolkit is built on Massachusetts law, which the ACLU says can be adapted for other states: The state’s high court has run years ahead of the US Supreme Court on digital privacy, requiring warrants for cell phone location data back in 2014.

sentences.en: If the approach sounds theoretical, Massachusetts's highest court has recently proven otherwise, twice.
sentences.zh: 如果这种方法听起来很理论上，那么马萨诸塞州最高法院最近两次证明了事实并非如此。

sentences.en: In 2024, the state’s Supreme Judicial Court permanently threw out drug and firearm charges against a Boston man—barring prosecutors from ever refiling them—after police defied a court order to turn over records of fake social media profiles that officers used to surveil him, built with predominantly Black and brown avatars.
sentences.zh: 2024 年，该州最高司法法院永久驳回了对一名波士顿男子的毒品和枪支指控，并禁止检察官重新提起指控，因为警方无视法院的命令，交出了警察用来监视他的虚假社交媒体资料记录，这些资料主要由黑人和棕色人种的头像组成。

sentences.en: Last year, the same court ruled that defendants can demand a hearing forcing prosecutors to prove ShotSpotter actually works before its evidence reaches a jury.
sentences.zh: 去年，同一法院裁定，被告可以要求举行听证会，迫使检察官在其证据送达陪审团之前证明 ShotSpotter 确实有效。

sentences.en: The rulings help explain why the toolkit is built on Massachusetts law, which the ACLU says can be adapted for other states: The state’s high court has run years ahead of the US Supreme Court on digital privacy, requiring warrants for cell phone location data back in 2014.
sentences.zh: 这些裁决有助于解释为什么该工具包是建立在马萨诸塞州法律的基础上的，美国公民自由联盟表示该法律可以适用于其他州：该州高等法院在数字隐私方面比美国最高法院领先多年，早在 2014 年就要求对手机位置数据提供搜查令。

blocks.html_en: The ACLU’s timing is not incidental: Three weeks ago, the US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that when police use a so-called geofence warrant—an order forcing Google to identify virtually all phones near a crime scene—they are conducting a “search” under the Fourth Amendment, triggering the Constitution's privacy protections. The justices sent the case back down for lower courts to work out what a valid geofence warrant looks like. The ACLU’s toolkit will ship a geofencing motion directly into those unsettled waters.

sentences.en: The ACLU’s timing is not incidental: Three weeks ago, the US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that when police use a so-called geofence warrant—an order forcing Google to identify virtually all phones near a crime scene—they are conducting a “search” under the Fourth Amendment, triggering the Constitution's privacy protections.
sentences.zh: 美国公民自由联盟的时机并非偶然：三周前，美国最高法院以 6 比 3 裁定，当警方使用所谓的地理围栏搜查令（一项迫使谷歌识别犯罪现场附近几乎所有手机的命令）时，他们正在根据第四修正案进行“搜查”，从而触发了宪法的隐私保护。

sentences.en: The justices sent the case back down for lower courts to work out what a valid geofence warrant looks like.
sentences.zh: 法官们将案件发回下级法院，以确定有效的地理围栏搜查令是什么样的。

sentences.en: The ACLU’s toolkit will ship a geofencing motion directly into those unsettled waters.
sentences.zh: 美国公民自由联盟的工具包将把地理围栏运动直接发送到那些不稳定的水域。

blocks.html_en: The motions are meant to be adapted and filed in real criminal cases, and the group is asking attorneys who use them to report back. The point, Herrmann says, is to take good lawyering that already exists and put it “at the fingertips of every attorney for every defendant,” while continuing to flag the gaps where new strategies are needed.

sentences.en: The motions are meant to be adapted and filed in real criminal cases, and the group is asking attorneys who use them to report back.
sentences.zh: 这些动议旨在在真实的刑事案件中进行调整和提交，该组织正在要求使用这些动议的律师进行报告。

sentences.en: The point, Herrmann says, is to take good lawyering that already exists and put it “at the fingertips of every attorney for every defendant,” while continuing to flag the gaps where new strategies are needed.
sentences.zh: 赫尔曼说，重点是利用现有的良好律师服务，并将其“置于每位被告的每位律师手中”，同时继续指出需要新策略的差距。

blocks.html_en: Updated at 1:10 pm ET, July 20, 2026: Added comments from Flock Safety and SoundThinking.

sentences.en: Updated at 1:10 pm ET, July 20, 2026: Added comments from Flock Safety and SoundThinking.
sentences.zh: 更新于 2026 年 7 月 20 日东部时间下午 1:10：添加了 Flock Safety 和 SoundThinking 的评论。

content_html_zh:
马萨诸塞州美国公民自由联盟表示，本周将向刑事辩护律师发布一个在线工具包，旨在揭露警方是否使用监控技术（面部识别、自动车牌读取器、枪击检测系统等）来秘密针对其客户立案。

该组织称该工具包是同类中的第一个工具包，它是围绕手工制作的法律动议构建的，当法官批准这些动议时，将迫使检察官披露是否对被告使用了监控技术。 它涵盖了一系列技术，从商业经纪人出售的“黄貂鱼”电话跟踪器和位置数据，到人工智能起草的警方报告和破解电话并从汽车信息娱乐系统中提取数据的取证工具。

它还包括针对政府机构和私人供应商的保存动议，要求在自动删除监控数据之前对其进行保存。 这些文件基于 1963 年制定的美国刑法的基本规则，即检察官必须移交所有有助于辩护的证据。 马萨诸塞州美国公民自由联盟表示，这些动议将通过受密码保护的图书馆分发，仅向经过验证的辩护律师开放。

马萨诸塞州 ACLU 的专职律师、该工具包的作者之一 Jennifer Herrmann 表示，该组织正在两条战线上与不受监管的监控工具进行斗争。 一方面是向城市施压，要求它们彻底放弃 Flock（车牌读取器网络）和 ShotSpotter（枪击检测系统）等工具。 另一个是武装辩护律师，在法庭上揭露监视行为。

赫尔曼说：“从最初使用技术和调查到真正进入法庭并提出宪法挑战可能需要很长的时间。”因此，这些动议为律师提供了一种“挑战过去已经发生的事情”的方法，建立一个可以延续到接下来出现的任何工具的记录。

该项目回应了其作者所描述的从警察采用新监控工具到法院最终决定使用它是否合法之间的长期滞后。 到 20 世纪 90 年代末，大多数美国人都携带手机，但美国最高法院直到 2018 年才要求警方获得手机位置记录搜查令。 这种滞后不仅仅是法庭行动缓慢的结果。 披露记录最差的技术面临的法庭裁决最少，正是因为被告无法质疑他们从未被告知的工具。

马萨诸塞州美国公民自由联盟在发布的材料中表示，警方部署这些工具“有时甚至没有向处理案件的检察官透露其用途”。 多年来，联邦调查局要求当地警察部门在使用手机基站模拟器之前签署保密协议——手提箱大小的设备，通常称为黄貂鱼，可以冒充手机信号塔来秘密定位手机。 在巴尔的摩，一项此类协议指示检察官完全撤销刑事案件，而不是在法庭上披露该设备，尽管该市警方在 2007 年至 2015 年间使用该设备超过 4,300 次。 ACLU 后来获得的 FBI 记录显示，这些协议至少持续到 2020 年。

当密苏里州调查人员在调查 2017 年的一起谋杀案时，使用 Fog Reveal（一种利用应用程序收集的位置数据来绘制人们活动轨迹的商业工具）时，美联社在帮助对受害者妻子定罪的审判证物中没有发现该工具的踪迹。 据《华盛顿邮报》报道，当路易斯安那州当局于 2022 年对兰德尔·古兰·里德 (Randal Quran Reid) 发出逮捕令时——一名佐治亚州男子因面部识别被误认，并被判入狱近一周——据《华盛顿邮报》报道，该逮捕令将他的身份识别归功于“可靠的消息来源”，而不是软件。 授权逮捕他的文件本身就掩盖了警方是如何怀疑他的。

该工具包的作者对这个问题的描述略有不同：他们主要担心的不仅是检察官隐藏的证据，还有从未被告知监控工具接触过他们案件的被告，以及不知道询问的辩护律师。

公民自由组织的技术自由政策顾问、该工具包的另一位作者吉迪恩·爱泼斯坦 (Gideon Epstein) 指出了马萨诸塞州自己的主要车牌阅读器案件“联邦诉美国”案。 麦卡锡案中，辩方只寻求少数摄像机的记录，而不是全州网络的记录。 “很多时候，他们并没有要求提供正在使用的监控技术的全部范围，”爱泼斯坦说。 他补充说，这些动议既是为了促使律师提出一切要求，也是为了迫使政府承认这一点。

保存动议针对的是一个单独且更微妙的问题：监视证据会自我毁灭。 Axon 的 Draft One 是一款人工智能工具，可以根据随身摄像机的音频编写警方报告，在设计上抛弃了机器编写的初稿。 2025 年 7 月，Axon 的一位产品经理告诉电子前沿基金会，这一选择可以避免给警方客户和检察官带来“披露难题”。 加州立法者对此事有不同的看法，并于去年颁布了一项法律，要求各机构保留原始草案。

在其他地方，Flock Safety 的车牌读取器网络默认会在 30 天后删除其录像，而芝加哥的 ShotSpotter 枪击检测系统合同则声明枪击数据是供应商自己的财产，而不是该市的财产。 在每种情况下，在指定辩护律师处理案件之前，被告反击索赔所需的数字材料可能会消失，或者超出政府的控制范围。

Flock Safety 发言人 Paris Lewbel 表示，该公司拥有保护系统中存储数据完整性和真实性的程序，以及确认 ALPR 图像或调查报告自从平台上撤下后未被更改的工具。

Lewbel 表示 Flock 遵守对客户数据的法律要求。 不具有法律约束力的请求 Flock 会向客户发出通知，并通知客户。

ShotSpotter 制造商 SoundThinking 的发言人表示，无论哪一方发出传票和法院命令，该公司都会遵守传票和法院命令，并配合刑事诉讼中索取数据的“合理要求”。 当传票涵盖公司认为机密的材料（例如其自己的商业秘密）时，它会要求法院限制该材料的传播。

爱泼斯坦谨慎地指出，美国公民自由联盟仍然普遍希望这些数据迅速消失。 它正在游说马萨诸塞州将车牌读取器的保留时间从 Flock 的默认保留时间 30 天缩短到两天。 但他表示，一旦有人面临指控，同样的数据就可以成为洗清罪名的证据，而这正是保存动议旨在保护的内容。

如果这种方法听起来很理论上，那么马萨诸塞州最高法院最近两次证明了事实并非如此。 2024 年，该州最高司法法院永久驳回了对一名波士顿男子的毒品和枪支指控，并禁止检察官重新提起指控，因为警方无视法院的命令，交出了警察用来监视他的虚假社交媒体资料记录，这些资料主要由黑人和棕色人种的头像组成。 去年，同一法院裁定，被告可以要求举行听证会，迫使检察官在其证据送达陪审团之前证明 ShotSpotter 确实有效。 这些裁决有助于解释为什么该工具包是建立在马萨诸塞州法律的基础上的，美国公民自由联盟表示该法律可以适用于其他州：该州高等法院在数字隐私方面比美国最高法院领先多年，早在 2014 年就要求对手机位置数据提供搜查令。

美国公民自由联盟的时机并非偶然：三周前，美国最高法院以 6 比 3 裁定，当警方使用所谓的地理围栏搜查令（一项迫使谷歌识别犯罪现场附近几乎所有手机的命令）时，他们正在根据第四修正案进行“搜查”，从而触发了宪法的隐私保护。 法官们将案件发回下级法院，以确定有效的地理围栏搜查令是什么样的。 美国公民自由联盟的工具包将把地理围栏运动直接发送到那些不稳定的水域。

这些动议旨在在真实的刑事案件中进行调整和提交，该组织正在要求使用这些动议的律师进行报告。 赫尔曼说，重点是利用现有的良好律师服务，并将其“置于每位被告的每位律师手中”，同时继续指出需要新策略的差距。

更新于 2026 年 7 月 20 日东部时间下午 1:10：添加了 Flock Safety 和 SoundThinking 的评论。
